June 7, 2026 AI + PMM

The Human Premium

We spent a year building an AI curriculum for product marketers at SAP. What we learned had almost nothing to do with the technology.

Last month I sat through a finance review where a guy named Stefan—I won't use his last name—had built an entire dashboard around our AI token consumption. Cost per query, queries per user, projected spend at scale. The spreadsheet had conditional formatting and color-coded alerts for when we exceeded thresholds. He'd clearly spent real time on it.

We spent forty-five minutes on that dashboard. Nobody asked whether any of us were actually getting better at our jobs.

I've been thinking about that meeting ever since, because it captures something broken about how most companies are approaching AI right now. They're treating it like a utility to meter—watching the dials, counting the kilowatt-hours—instead of a capability to build. The gap between those two framings is where all the value is hiding.

The Curriculum

For the past year, my team has been building something unusual—an eight-unit curriculum that teaches product marketers how to work with AI. Not a lunch-and-learn. Not a prompt engineering cheat sheet someone printed off Reddit. An actual learning program with readings, exercises, hands-on labs, and assessments that tell you whether you learned anything.

We built it because we kept watching the same pattern repeat. Someone would discover Claude or ChatGPT, get excited, produce a few things with it, and then plateau. The output was faster—maybe even fine—but it wasn't better. It was just more. More mediocrity at higher velocity.

The marketers who broke through that plateau had something the others didn't. It wasn't better prompts. It wasn't fancier tools. It was better foundations. They understood positioning frameworks cold—not just April Dunford's boxes, but why the boxes work the way they do. They had a mental model for buyer psychology that didn't come from a blog post. They'd been in enough rooms to know when a messaging deck was going to die on contact with a customer.

When those people used AI, something different happened. The AI became a sparring partner, not a slot machine. They'd push back on its suggestions. Catch its blind spots. Use it to stress-test their thinking instead of outsource it.

The output sounded like them. Not like everyone else.

"AI doesn't make you better at positioning. It makes you faster at whatever you already are."

That observation—half mine, half stolen from a conversation with someone on my team—became the thesis of the whole curriculum. AI amplifies capability. Strong foundations plus AI equals sharper, faster work. Weak foundations plus AI equals mediocrity at scale, and you won't even know it's mediocre because you don't have the frame of reference to judge.

What the Assessments Showed

We built assessments into every unit. Not quizzes about AI—assessments about the underlying craft. Can you identify weak positioning? Can you spot the gap between a feature description and a value proposition? Can you construct a competitive narrative that doesn't just inventory capabilities like a parts list?

The results were humbling. Tool access was basically universal—everyone had Copilot, most had ChatGPT, some had Claude. But actual capability followed a power law. A small percentage were using AI to genuinely elevate their strategic work. The majority were using it as a slightly faster search engine with better prose.

The gap wasn't about AI fluency. It was about PMM fluency. The people who couldn't construct a good positioning statement by hand also couldn't prompt their way to one. The people who understood the frameworks could use AI to explore ten variations in the time it used to take to write one—and they could tell you which three were worth keeping and why.

The Co-Author Gap

Most companies frame AI as something you delegate to. Write me a positioning statement. Draft me a product description. Generate some ad copy. It's task completion. Outsourcing with extra steps.

The marketers who break through are doing something different. They treat AI as something you think with. They don't ask it to write the positioning—they use it to pressure-test their positioning against competitive alternatives, identify holes in their messaging hierarchy, challenge their assumptions about what the buyer actually cares about.

That's not delegation. That's collaboration. And collaboration requires a human who can hold up their end of the conversation.

I've started calling it the co-author gap. The distance between using AI as a content vending machine and using it as a thought partner. Almost everyone is still on the vending machine side—insert prompt, receive output, move on. The few who cross over invested in their own capability first. They had something worth collaborating on.

Stefan's Dashboard

So back to that finance meeting.

Stefan's dashboard was immaculate—the token tracking, the cost projections, the per-user breakdowns, all of it technically correct. And all of it measuring exactly the wrong thing. He was tracking consumption when he should have been tracking capability, counting inputs when the only thing that matters is output.

The constraint isn't the cost of AI. Tokens are cheap and getting cheaper. The constraint is the cost of human capability. Every dollar you spend building real skills delivers multiples in AI ROI. Every dollar you save by skipping training gets wasted on tools that people don't know how to use well.

The companies that figure this out will build an advantage that compounds. Better people using AI get more productive, which frees them up to learn more, which makes them better at using AI. Flywheel. But you have to kick-start it with genuine capability building, not just license procurement.

The companies that keep watching Stefan's dashboard will optimize their API costs and lose the race entirely.

What We Built

The curriculum is live—eight units, forty-plus assessments, seven hands-on AI labs. We've put a few dozen people through it so far. The ones who engage seriously come out different. Not because they learned AI tricks, but because they rebuilt their foundations and then discovered what AI can actually do when you know what you're asking for.

We're opening it up more broadly now. If you want to see what a curriculum-based approach to AI enablement looks like—not just for PMMs, but as a model for this kind of capability building—the whole thing is at futureofpmm.com.

The human premium is real, and right now almost nobody's investing in it.
